findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly how `_load_acli` delegates to `select_backend` while keeping its module-shaped return value: e.g., the call sites at `applier.py:439` and `fetcher.py:367` should call `select_backend(config)` directly (replacing the two-step `_load_acli()` → `.AcliClient(...)` pattern), and `_load_acli` should be left as a stub that just returns the `acli` module (still patchable); the AC should be updated to reflect that `_load_acli` itself is NOT changed, only the sites that follow it.
evidence: applier.py:224-228: `_load_acli()` returns `acli` module; caller at applier.py:439 does `acli_mod = _load_acli()` then `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)`
fetcher.py:113-117: same pattern — returns `acli` module; caller at fetcher.py:367 does `acli_mod = _load_acli()` then `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)`
Plan AC: 'the existing tests that patch them still pass unchanged' — requires the seam interface to be backward-compatible
Backend (S2) is a facade object, not a module — it does not have an .AcliClient attribute
findings.finding: The `_load_acli()` seam in both `applier.py` and `fetcher.py` currently returns the `acli` MODULE (callers do `acli_mod.AcliClient(...)`); the plan says the functions will 'delegate to `select_backend`' while existing tests 'still pass unchanged'. These goals are mechanically incompatible: `select_backend()` returns a `Backend` object, not a module with an `.AcliClient` attribute, so tests patching `_load_acli` to return a fake module with `.AcliClient` would break unless the delegation wrapper reconstructs the module-shaped interface — which the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Approach section: 'keep the functions, have them delegate to select_backend'; AC line 4: 'existing tests that patch them still pass unchanged'
scenarios: Implementer changes _load_acli to return `select_backend(config)`, breaks every existing test that patches `_load_acli` with a fake module (AttributeError on .AcliClient).
Implementer adds an adapter shim making Backend look like a module-with-AcliClient, but this is undocumented and fragile.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement the import-trigger mechanism that ensures `adapters/jira/backend.py` (and its `@register` side effect) is loaded before any `select_backend()` call — either by having `_backend_registry.py` explicitly import all built-in adapters, or by having the reconciler package `__init__.py` / entry point trigger the adapter import — and add an AC verifying the registry is non-empty in a cold-import scenario.
evidence: Search for `_backend` and `backend.py` imports in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` returned no matches — `adapters/jira/backend.py` is not yet imported by any reconciler module.
The call sites (`applier.py:443`, `applier.py:605`, `fetcher.py:371`, `run_differs.py:403`, `_attestation.py:82`, `acli.py:145`) do not currently import `adapters/jira/backend.py`.
Plan states 'import-time registration; no entry-point plugins yet' but is silent on what code path ensures the `@register` side-effect fires before `select_backend()` is invoked.
The idempotency unit test ('the registry is idempotent under repeated import') presupposes the import happens, but does not address the cold-start case.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `adapters/jira/backend.py` is decorated with `@register('jira', ...)` and that registration happens at import time, but does not specify which module triggers the import of `adapters/jira/backend.py` before `select_backend(config)` is first called. The existing call-site modules (`acli.py`, `applier.py`, `fetcher.py`, `run_differs.py`, `_attestation.py`) currently have no import of `adapters/jira/backend.py`, so if `_backend_registry.py` itself does not explicitly import it, the registry will be empty at resolution time.
findings.location: Edit-set: 'adapters/jira/backend.py — decorate JiraBackend with @register("jira", …)'; Approach: 'import-time registration'
scenarios: At runtime, `select_backend(config)` is called with `config.reconciler.backend = 'jira'`, but the registry dict is empty because `adapters/jira/backend.py` was never imported — the call raises an 'unknown backend' config error for the default value.
The 'unknown-key' unit test passes (because that test explicitly imports the adapter first), masking the cold-start empty-registry bug in production.
findings.suggested_fix: Have `_backend_registry.py` include a `_load_builtin_backends()` function (called at module bottom or lazily in `select_backend`) that imports `adapters.jira.backend`; this keeps the registry self-contained and mirrors how `typed_dispatch._build_leaves()` populates itself eagerly at module import time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the import-ordering contract: document (or enforce via explicit import in `_backend_registry.py` or the config module) that `adapters/jira/backend.py` is imported before any config validation runs, so the registry always contains `"jira"` when the default value is validated.
evidence: "validated against the registry's known keys" (config.py + schema section)
"import-time registration" (Approach section)
"decorate `JiraBackend` with `@register("jira", …)`" (Edit-set, adapters/jira/backend.py)
Registry is a module-level dict populated by an idempotent `register` decorator (Approach) — it is empty until `adapters/jira/backend.py` is imported; no import ordering contract is stated.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `config.reconciler.backend` is validated against the registry's known keys at config-load time, but the registry is only populated when `adapters/jira/backend.py` is imported (import-time registration). If config validation is triggered before that import has occurred — e.g., during a cold startup where config is parsed before the jira adapter module is on the import path — the registry will be empty and the default value `"jira"` will fail its own validation. The plan does not specify who is responsible for ensuring the jira adapter is imported before any config validation runs, nor does it define an import ordering contract.
findings.location: Context / Problem — `_backend_registry.py` design; AC line: "`config.reconciler.backend` exists, defaults to `"jira"`, and rejects an unregistered value with an error listing the registered keys`"
scenarios: Any harness startup (library, CLI, or MCP server) that loads and validates config before importing `adapters/jira/backend.py` will see an empty registry and reject `reconciler.backend = "jira"` with a spurious 'unregistered key' error, breaking initialization under the default config.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend_registry.py`, add a deferred-validation mode: rather than checking against registered keys at config-parse time, validate at `select_backend()` call time (when the registry is guaranteed populated). Alternatively, have `_backend_registry.py` itself import `adapters/jira/backend` unconditionally at the bottom of the file to guarantee registration before any consumer can call `select_backend` or validate config.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify WHERE `reconciler.backend` validation fires: either (a) defer to `select_backend()` (clean, avoids dep cycle — update AC to say 'select_backend raises a ConfigError') or (b) hardcode the allowed set as a string literal in `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` and keep the registry check only in `select_backend()` — do NOT import the registry from `_config_schema.py`.
evidence: Plan: 'add the reconciler.backend key, default "jira", validated against the registry's known keys'
_config_schema.py line 11: 'Imports only stdlib — a low-level leaf with no rebar.* deps'
_SECTIONS['reconciler'] in _config_schema.py lines 649-655 uses only stdlib coercers; all existing choice-validated keys (_as_choice) have hardcoded allowed-set literals, not runtime registry lookups
Validation against a dynamically-populated registry requires importing the registry module, which imports adapters/jira/backend.py → acli.py → acli_subprocess.py — all non-stdlib
findings.finding: The plan says `reconciler.backend` is 'validated against the registry's known keys', but `_config_schema.py` is explicitly a stdlib-only, zero-rebar-deps leaf — importing the registry (which imports `adapters/jira/backend.py` → `acli.py`) into the schema coercion layer introduces a forbidden dependency cycle. The plan does not specify whether validation happens at schema-parse time (impossible without a dep cycle) or deferred to `select_backend()` (valid, but a meaningful design decision that changes the AC wording).
findings.location: Edit-set: `config.py` (+ its schema) section; AC line 1: 'rejects an unregistered value with an error listing the registered keys'
scenarios: Implementer adds `lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, _backend_registry.registered_keys())` to _SECTIONS['reconciler'], breaking the 'stdlib only' invariant and potentially causing circular imports on config load.
Implementer defers validation to select_backend() (correct) but the AC says 'verified by unit tests' against the config key — leading to test confusion about where the error fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `lambda v, k: _as_str(v, k)` in `_SECTIONS['reconciler']` (accepts any string at parse time), and have `select_backend(config)` look up `config.reconciler.backend` in the registry dict and raise `ConfigError(f'reconciler.backend: unknown backend {key!r}; registered: {sorted(registry.keys())}')` if absent. This keeps the schema a stdlib-only leaf and puts registry-aware validation exactly where the registry is available.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_jira_wiring.py` (lines 173–180) as a consumer affected by the `_attestation.py:82` change, and specify whether `build_acli_client_from_env` will return the raw `AcliClient` (via `backend.transport` accessor) or a facade — so the existing `.jira_url` assertion remains valid or is explicitly updated.
evidence: tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_jira_wiring.py:173: client = _attestation.build_acli_client_from_env()
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_jira_wiring.py:174: assert client.jira_url == 'https://j.example'

Plan: '_attestation.py:82 (build_acli_client_from_env() at _attestation.py:41). Each replaces the inline AcliClient build with select_backend(config) (or, for _attestation.py, returns the configured backend’s transport).'
findings.finding: The existing unit test `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_jira_wiring.py` calls `_attestation.build_acli_client_from_env()` and asserts `client.jira_url == 'https://j.example'` on the returned object. The plan rewires `_attestation.py:82` to 'return the configured backend's transport' rather than an `AcliClient`. If the returned object is a `Backend` or `TicketTransport` facade rather than an `AcliClient`, `.jira_url` won't exist and the test will break. This consumer is not enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: Edit-set / inline construction sites — _attestation.py:82; Approach section
scenarios: After the change, build_acli_client_from_env() returns a Backend/TicketTransport; test_config_jira_wiring.py:174 asserts .jira_url on it and raises AttributeError, breaking make test.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
verify command `grep` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen AC5 with a concrete proving command that compares a structured artifact (e.g. `jq -e '.events | length'` on the fixture output) against a pre-captured baseline, and specify what 'identical outcomes' means (same event types, same field values, same count).
evidence: 'verified by the integration/characterization test' — no command is given
'identical outcomes' does not specify what artifact is compared, by what command, or what the expected value/count is
Verify-command defect taxonomy item 4 (fixture invalidity/underspecification) and item 5 (missing cardinality assertion) apply
findings.finding: AC5 ('identical outcomes to pre-story main') lacks a concrete proving command and uses vague equality — no assertion artifact (event list diff, structured output, count) is specified, making it unmeasurable in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'An end-to-end reconcile pass on the fixture store yields identical outcomes to pre-story main under the default config'
scenarios: An executor writes a test that checks only that the reconcile pass does not raise an exception, marks it green, and considers AC5 satisfied — the behavioral equivalence claim is never actually measured.
The pre-story baseline is never captured, so 'identical' has no reference point.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'verified by the integration/characterization test' with: 'verified by `pytest tests/integration/test_reconcile_fixture.py::test_outcome_stable -v` which asserts `events == FIXTURE_BASELINE` where `FIXTURE_BASELINE` is a checked-in JSON snapshot of the pre-story reconcile output (generated once and committed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the 'Teeth' test to an explicit Acceptance Criterion (e.g. 'Setting `config.reconciler.backend` to a registered `FakeBackend` key causes `select_backend` to return the fake and the reconcile pass invokes the fake's transport — verified by a unit/integration test asserting the fake's call count > 0').
evidence: The 'Teeth' bullet is in Testing, not in ACs
No AC reads: 'setting config.reconciler.backend to a FakeBackend key causes select_backend to drive the fake transport'
E6 checklist item: 'every acceptance criterion maps to at least one described step' — the converse is also problematic: the most discriminating test has no AC to anchor it
findings.finding: The 'teeth' test — the only criterion that proves backend selection is actually honored end-to-end (FakeBackend is driven instead of JiraBackend) — appears only in the Testing section, not in the Acceptance Criteria, so there is no AC that an executor must satisfy to demonstrate real selection behavior.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Teeth: point the config at a FakeBackend key and assert the pass drives the fake's transport instead of Jira'; Acceptance Criteria (absence)
scenarios: An executor routes all 6 sites through select_backend but wires select_backend to always return JiraBackend regardless of config; every non-Teeth test passes; the AC list is satisfied; the selection mechanism is broken but undetected.
A reviewer signs off on ACs without realising the FakeBackend exercise is not required by any AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] With `config.reconciler.backend = "fake"` (a registered `FakeBackend`), `select_backend(config)` returns the fake and a reconcile pass invokes `FakeBackend.transport` at least once (verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_backend_registry.py::test_teeth` asserting `fake_backend.transport.call_count > 0`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a registry reset/cleanup mechanism for tests (e.g., a context manager, a `_reset()` helper, or test fixture that saves and restores the registry dict) so that `FakeBackend` registration in the Teeth test does not leak into other test assertions about registered key names.
evidence: "point the config at a `FakeBackend` key and assert the pass drives the fake's transport" (Testing, Teeth bullet)
"Registry is a module-level dict" (Approach)
No deregistration or test-teardown mechanism is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
AC: `select_backend` raises a config error 'naming registered keys' — this message content changes if FakeBackend persists across tests.
findings.finding: The module-level registry dict is process-lifetime shared state with no cleanup or reset mechanism. The 'Teeth' test registers a `FakeBackend` under a new key (e.g., `"fake"`) to verify end-to-end selection. Once registered, that key persists in the dict for the entire test session. The plan specifies idempotency under repeated import but does not define a deregistration or test-fixture teardown path. If other tests assert the exact set of registered keys (e.g., error messages listing registered keys), the leaked `FakeBackend` key will corrupt those assertions unless tests run in a specific order.
findings.location: Testing — 'Teeth' bullet; Approach — 'Registry is a module-level dict populated by an idempotent `register` decorator'
scenarios: In a test session where the Teeth test runs before the 'unknown-key error names registered keys' unit test, the error message will include `"fake"` in the list of registered keys, potentially causing the unit test to fail if it asserts an exact string or key set.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_reset_for_testing()` function to `_backend_registry.py` that replaces the dict with a copy of the initial state (or clears it), and wire it into a pytest fixture with `autouse=False` for tests that register fake backends.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the seam strategy for `run_differs.py:403` explicitly: either introduce a `_load_acli` wrapper in `run_differs.py` (matching applier/fetcher) and note it as a new seam, or document that this site switches to a direct `select_backend(config)` call with no preserved seam (and confirm no existing tests rely on patching `run_differs.acli`).
evidence: Grep of `run_differs.py` for `_load_acli` returns no matches — no such function exists in that file
`run_differs.py:399`: `from rebar_reconciler import acli as acli_mod_for_comments` (direct import, no seam wrapper)
Grep of `tests/` for `run_differs.*_load_acli` returns no matches — no test patches this seam
Tests for run_differs patch `_load` (the generic sibling loader) or sub-functions, not a dedicated `_load_acli` seam
findings.finding: The plan asserts that only `applier._load_acli` and `fetcher._load_acli` are the test seams to preserve, but `run_differs.py:403` constructs an `AcliClient` via a bare `from rebar_reconciler import acli as acli_mod_for_comments` inline import — there is no `_load_acli` wrapper function in `run_differs.py` and no test patches one. The plan's seam-preservation strategy ('keep the functions, have them delegate to `select_backend`') does not cover this site.
findings.location: Context / Problem section ('Note `applier._load_acli` / `fetcher._load_acli` are patched seams in the tests') and AC bullet 4 ('The `applier._load_acli` / `fetcher._load_acli` test seams still exist')
scenarios: The implementer rewires `applier._load_acli` and `fetcher._load_acli` correctly but misses that `run_differs.py:403` needs a different migration strategy (either a new `_load_acli` seam or direct `select_backend(config)` call) since no wrapper seam exists to delegate through.

AC grep check ('no remaining direct `AcliClient(` construction outside `adapters/jira/`') passes only if `run_differs.py:403` is also migrated — but the seam strategy described in the plan doesn't cover it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the circular import at `acli.py:145`: since `acli.py` IS the Jira transport module (it lives inside the package root, not under `adapters/jira/`), consider whether this site is already 'inside `adapters/jira/`' for the purposes of the grep AC, or specify that `_backend_registry` must use deferred/lazy imports for `JiraBackend` registration to avoid the cycle.
evidence: acli.py:145: `client = AcliClient(...)` inside `transition_issue()` — this is a function WITHIN acli.py itself
Plan: `adapters/jira/backend.py — decorate JiraBackend with @register('jira', …)` — JiraBackend wraps acli.AcliClient
Circular chain: acli.py → _backend_registry (import-time registration) → adapters/jira/backend.py → acli.AcliClient → acli.py
acli.py line 24-58: acli.py already imports several sibling modules at module level; adding a registry import would execute registration side-effects at acli import time
findings.finding: Routing `acli.py:145` (the `transition_issue()` free function inside `acli.py`) through `select_backend()` creates a circular import: `acli.py` → `_backend_registry` → `adapters/jira/backend.py` → `JiraBackend` wraps `acli.AcliClient` → imports `acli.py`. The plan does not acknowledge or resolve this cycle.
findings.location: Edit-set: 'The inline construction sites — enumerated concretely: acli.py:145'
scenarios: Implementer adds `from rebar_reconciler._backend_registry import select_backend` at the top of acli.py — circular ImportError at startup.

Implementer uses a local/deferred import inside transition_issue() — silently works but is not specified; also, if _backend_registry itself imports acli at module level for registration, the cycle still bites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the '5 sites' claim in the title, Context, and Approach with the six sites enumerated in Context and AC3 — update all occurrences to 'six' so the count is consistent throughout the plan.

findings.finding: The plan title and Problem section say '5 inline AcliClient constructions' and the Approach repeats 'The 5 call sites', but the enumeration in Context and AC3 both list 6 distinct sites (`acli.py:145`, `applier.py:443`, `applier.py:605`, `fetcher.py:371`, `run_differs.py:403`, `_attestation.py:82`). findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unsupported 'already used elsewhere in rebar' claim with a specific citation of the closest existing analogous pattern (e.g., `typed_dispatch._build_leaves`, `rebar.metrics.registry.REGISTRY`) or acknowledge this is the first decorator-registry in the reconciler and note why that is still the right choice.
evidence: Search for `@register`, `def register(`, and `decorator-registry` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` returned zero matches for a class-factory decorator pattern.
Existing `rebar.metrics` `register()` is a plain list-append for metric specs, not a class-factory decorator-registry.
`typed_dispatch._build_leaves()` is a static dict populated at module import, not a decorator-based registration pattern.
Plan quote: 'mirroring the in-tree decorator-registry pattern already used elsewhere in rebar (not a setuptools entry-point system)'
findings.finding: The plan asserts the in-tree decorator-registry is 'already used elsewhere in rebar' as justification for the pattern choice, but a codebase search finds no matching decorator-based class-factory registry in the reconciler package or adjacent modules — the existing patterns (metrics `register()`, `typed_dispatch._build_leaves()`, `_deprecations._ENTRIES` tuple) are structurally different. The stated prior-art justification is unverified.
findings.location: Approach section: 'mirroring the in-tree decorator-registry pattern already used elsewhere in rebar'
scenarios: During implementation, the author discovers no existing pattern to mirror and must design the decorator API from scratch, potentially producing a design inconsistent with the codebase's actual idioms.
A reviewer or future contributor cannot locate the 'existing pattern' the plan references, reducing confidence in the design rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact Backend attribute or method the `_attestation.py` site should call (e.g. `select_backend(config).transport`) so the instruction is self-contained without consulting S2.
evidence: All other 5 sites say 'select_backend(config)' uniformly; _attestation.py diverges with 'the configured backend's transport' without naming the attribute
The Backend facade is defined in S2 (prerequisite), so the attribute name is not available in this plan
findings.finding: The `_attestation.py:82` routing instruction says 'returns the configured backend's transport' rather than 'call `select_backend(config)`', leaving the exact API surface undefined — an executor cannot determine which attribute/method of Backend to call without consulting S2.
findings.location: Edit-set / inline construction sites description — '_attestation.py:82 (build_acli_client_from_env() at _attestation.py:41). Each replaces … or, for _attestation.py, returns the configured backend's transport.'
scenarios: An executor guesses `backend.transport`, another guesses `backend.get_transport()`, producing inconsistent or incorrect code without a clarifying round-trip.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'returns the configured backend's transport' with 'calls `select_backend(config).transport` (or whichever attribute the S2 `Backend` facade exposes for raw transport access) and returns it'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan (e.g., 'revert the registry module and restore the six inline constructions via git revert <sha>') or describe an expand-contract sequencing (e.g., wire sites one at a time behind the registry, keeping the old path callable) so that rollback is an explicit, documented choice rather than implicit.
evidence: Approach: 'Behavior is unchanged: with the default config (reconciler.backend = "jira"), select_backend() returns a JiraBackend and every existing path behaves exactly as before.'
No back-out or rollback section exists in the plan.
AC5 verifies behavior preservation via integration test but names no rollback path if the integration test fails post-deploy.
findings.finding: The plan rewires all six production AcliClient construction sites simultaneously through a new registry, but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing for this change. The plan asserts 'Behavior is unchanged' and provides an integration test, but the T4 rubric requires an explicit reversibility mechanism beyond acknowledgement.
scenarios: A bug in the `select_backend` resolver or registry initialization breaks all six sites simultaneously at deploy time with no documented back-out procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the registry design decision (registry-vs-entry-points, YAGNI rationale, Backend facade as extension point) in an ADR or AGENTS.md section so future contributors have a durable rationale.
evidence: 'a second backend is added in-tree under adapters/<x>/' — YAGNI rationale is present in the plan body but not designated for capture in any durable artifact
'mirroring the in-tree decorator-registry pattern already used elsewhere in rebar' — the rationale for the choice is inline only
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update in the edit-set or scope section
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision (decorator-registry for backend selection, YAGNI stance on entry-point plugins, Backend facade as the extension point) but does not specify capturing this in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update, leaving future contributors without a durable rationale for why the registry was chosen over, e.g., a DI container or setuptools entry points.
scenarios: A future contributor wants to add a second backend and doesn't know whether to use entry-points or the registry; there is no ADR to consult.
A reviewer of adapters/jira/backend.py sees a `@register` decorator with no documented contract for what registration implies.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the edit-set: `docs/adr/NNN-backend-registry.md` (or an AGENTS.md section) covering: (1) why decorator-registry over setuptools entry-points, (2) the YAGNI boundary, (3) how to add a second in-tree backend, (4) the Backend facade contract reference (S2).

The plan's scope section states 'returns the `Backend` facade from S2' and instructs 'decorate `JiraBackend` with `@register("jira", …)`' — both committed work items rest on an artifact that does not yet exist.
